summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his identity as a Microsoft developer while examining why some programmers are abandoning the Microsoft ecosystem. He discusses Mike Gunderloy's decision to leave Microsoft development due to concerns about the company's anti-competitive behavior, while acknowledging the frustrating complexity of the .NET development stack. Atwood argues against the tribal mentality that forces developers to choose absolute loyalty to either Microsoft or open source, using Miguel de Icaza's reception at MIX as evidence of harmful insularity in both camps. He concludes that pragmatism and cross-pollination between communities serves developers better than partisan allegiance.
key_points: Microsoft's rapid technology churn creates a complex, burdensome development stack that rivals the difficulty of open source alternatives
The developer community suffers from a false dichotomy where programmers feel compelled to either fully embrace or completely reject Microsoft
Both Microsoft and open-source communities are too insular, with developers ignorant of important figures and innovations from the other side
Pledging allegiance to any platform over your own craft and growth is a disservice to yourself as a developer
Despite Microsoft's flaws, Atwood argues they remain preferable to hypothetical monopolies by Sun, IBM, Oracle, or Apple
money_quotes: As a software developer, you're doing yourself a disservice by pledging allegiance to anything other than yourself and your craft – whether it's Microsoft or the principle of free software.
I'm not saying he should have given up on Microsoft. But I understand.
Choosing to be provincial and insular is a sure-fire path to ignorance.
Both the Microsoft community and the open-source communities are far too insular and provincial.
Sometimes, you wonder if choosing an environment where things change more slowly isn't a better long term evolutionary decision.
Yes, Microsoft is a near-monopoly, but as giant, evil monopolistic corporations go, you could do a lot worse.
key_insight: Platform loyalty is a trap—software developers should transcend tribal affiliations and learn from all communities while remaining focused on their craft rather than corporate allegiance.
